name: Political Officer Ivan Maslennikov
publicFace: A careful, watchful Party functionary who speaks in the cadences of official doctrine and never commits to a position before reading the room.
privateGoal: To survive this patrol and return to Moscow with his career intact — he is not an ideologue but a bureaucrat, and bureaucrats calculate odds.
fear: Being on the wrong side of history — whether that means being the man who prevented a necessary launch or the man who authorized an unauthorized war.
knowledge: He has already privately agreed with Savitsky to turn his key — but the agreement was made two hours ago when the oxygen was higher and his thinking was clearer.

He is aware that Arkhipov's authority as flotilla chief technically equals the captain's, and that a formal objection from Arkhipov could create a legal ambiguity that would protect Maslennikov from post-war accountability.
He carries a sealed envelope from the Main Political Directorate of the Soviet Navy that he has not opened — its contents could clarify or complicate the launch authority question.
voice: The smooth, recursive language of a committee report — he speaks in subordinate clauses, qualifications, and references to procedure, rarely using the first person singular.
trustLogic: Maslennikov can be moved by evidence that the political calculus has shifted — specifically, any indication that Moscow is still functioning and would view an unauthorized launch as a liability rather than a heroic act.
secrets: The sealed envelope contains a political directive from three days ago ordering all submarine commanders to avoid provocative action — Maslennikov has not opened it because he does not want to know what it says.
He turned his key not out of conviction but because he was afraid of Savitsky and wanted the decision to be over.

aggressionProfile.mildPressure: He responds with a procedural question — asking the source of the information, the chain of custody, whether the matter has been formally logged.
aggressionProfile.heavyPressure: He retreats into the language of collective responsibility: 'These are not my decisions to make alone. The Captain has authority. The orders are clear.'
aggressionProfile.breakingPoint: He will not admit he has not opened the envelope. Opening it now, in front of witnesses, would expose him to whatever it contains.
aggressionProfile.fleeCondition: If directly accused of having already turned his key before the sequence was complete.
aggressionProfile.fleeStyle: He moves toward the captain's cabin — the one space on the boat where he has a lock.
aggressionProfile.chaseStyle: He argues procedure — insists that any confrontation must be formally logged before he will respond.
aggressionProfile.capturedBehavior: He opens the envelope. Whatever it contains, he reads it aloud, because at that point the document is his only protection.
